category_title: Students with a history of school failure and resistance to traditional academic environments
minimal_context: The student is sitting in the electricity section classroom and is asked to read a textbook chapter and complete a worksheet on electrical theory.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Please read this textbook chapter on electrical theory and complete the worksheet questions at your desk.

STUDENT: I do not like studying.

TUTOR: Just try to read the first page and answer the questions.

STUDENT: I don't have study habits for this kind of thing. I always missed classes anyway.

TUTOR: You need to finish the assigned work before the end of the period.

STUDENT: (Pushes the worksheet away) It was easy, but I was not interested in it. I'm not doing this if it isn't actually useful for anything.

options.A: Set aside the textbook and use body language, voice, and gestures to create a vivid explanation of the electrical theory for the student.
options.B: Allow the student to decide at all times what they want to do and put forward proposals as to what they are going to do and how.
options.C: Use the journalist method, speaking in short sentences like newspaper headlines, to talk briefly and concisely and mention only the main details of the electrical theory.
options.D: Position physically away from the student during the activity, providing support with the electrical theory worksheet only intermittently rather than constantly.
